_meta.authoringNotes: Clinical data cross-checked against 2015 ESC Guidelines on Pericardial Diseases (Adler et al. Eur Heart J 2015, PMID 26320112) and COPE/CORP/CORP-2 colchicine trials. Epidemiology from Imazio et al. Lancet 2015 and Ann Intern Med 2011 (PMID 21242236).

h1Title: Acute Pericarditis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute pericarditis is inflammation of the two-layered pericardial sac surrounding the heart, producing sharp chest pain that worsens on breathing and lying flat. It accounts for approximately 5% of emergency department presentations for non-ischaemic chest pain and is the most common pericardial disease in clinical practice. Most cases in developed countries are viral or idiopathic, though bacterial, autoimmune, and neoplastic causes become proportionally more important as disease recurs or fails to respond to initial therapy. Aspirin or NSAIDs plus colchicine, introduced by the ESC 2015 guidelines as standard care, reduce the risk of recurrence from approximately 30% to 10-15%. The outlook for idiopathic or viral pericarditis is excellent; fewer than 2% progress to constrictive pericarditis.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated incidence 27.7 per 100,000 person-years in population-based studies (Imazio 2015); accounts for 5% of ED chest pain presentations
keyStats.demographics: Most common in men aged 20-50; male-to-female ratio approximately 2:1 in idiopathic disease; no strong ethnic predisposition
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in young and middle-aged adults aged 20-50 years
keyStats.globalCases: Global burden not precisely quantified; higher in sub-Saharan Africa and India where tuberculosis is the dominant aetiology

definition: Acute pericarditis (ICD-10: I30.9) is diagnosed clinically when at least two of the following four criteria are present: typical pericarditic chest pain (sharp, pleuritic, positional), a pericardial friction rub on auscultation, new widespread saddle-shaped ST elevation or PR depression on ECG, and a new or enlarging pericardial effusion on echocardiography (ESC 2015). Elevated inflammatory markers — C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and white cell count — support the diagnosis but are not required. The pathophysiology involves activation of the pericardial mesothelial cells and underlying epicardium by an infectious or immune trigger, releasing pro-inflammatory cytokines and recruiting neutrophils and macrophages into the pericardial space. Fluid accumulation is variable: most episodes cause only a small effusion, but rapid accumulation above 250-300 mL can produce haemodynamic compromise (cardiac tamponade). Pericarditis is classified by timing: acute (<4-6 weeks), incessant (>4-6 weeks without remission), recurrent (recurrence after a symptom-free interval of at least 4-6 weeks), and chronic (>3 months). It is managed primarily by cardiologists, with rheumatology, infectious disease, or oncology input for specific aetiologies.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for 70-90% of acute pericarditis in Western countries. No specific viral cause identified in most cases; suspected enterovirus, adenovirus, or other common respiratory viruses. Excellent prognosis; recurrence in 15-30% without colchicine, dropping to ~8% with colchicine (COPE trial).

Dominant aetiology in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia (up to 90% of pericarditis in HIV-endemic areas). Presents subacutely with fever, night sweats, and large exudative effusion. High risk of constrictive pericarditis (up to 40%) and pericardial tamponade; managed with anti-tubercular therapy plus adjunct corticosteroids.

Rare but life-threatening. Usually Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pneumoniae via haematogenous spread, direct extension from pneumonia, or post-cardiac surgery. Presents with high fever, severe illness, and rapidly accumulating effusion requiring urgent pericardiocentesis and IV antibiotics.

Complicates systemic lupus erythematosus (pericarditis in 25-40% of SLE patients), rheumatoid arthritis, systemic sclerosis, and Still's disease. Elevated anti-dsDNA or ANA antibodies. Managed with disease-modifying therapy alongside colchicine.

Immune-mediated pericarditis arising 1-4 weeks after cardiac surgery, MI (Dressler syndrome), catheter ablation, or pericardiocentesis. Antinuclear and anti-heart antibodies often detectable. Treated with aspirin, NSAIDs, and colchicine.

primarySymptoms: Sharp, stabbing chest pain aggravated by inspiration and coughing and relieved by leaning forward (characteristic postural modification distinguishing pericarditis from MI), localised to the retrosternal area or left precordium.
Radiation of pain to the left trapezius ridge — a highly specific sign reflecting phrenic nerve irritation by pericardial inflammation along the lateral diaphragm.
Worsening of symptoms when lying flat (recumbent position increases cardiac mass pressure on the pericardium) and relief when sitting upright and leaning forward.

Pericardial friction rub heard on auscultation — a high-pitched, scratching, multi-component sound best heard at the lower left sternal border in the sitting-forward position; present in approximately 60-70% of cases but may be intermittent.
Fever between 38 and 39°C in most viral and idiopathic cases; higher fever (above 38.5°C) raises concern for bacterial or autoimmune aetiology.
Dyspnoea from pericardial effusion compressing adjacent lung parenchyma or from pain-splinted breathing in severe pleurisy.
Palpitations or awareness of heart rate irregularity from atrial irritation adjacent to the inflamed pericardium, especially with larger effusions.
General malaise, fatigue, and myalgia in the days preceding chest pain onset, consistent with a viral prodrome in idiopathic pericarditis.
Persistent cough or hiccup from diaphragmatic or phrenic nerve irritation in large pericardial effusions.
earlyWarningSigns: Viral upper respiratory or gastrointestinal illness 1-2 weeks before onset of chest pain — the characteristic viral prodrome of acute pericarditis.
Pleuritic chest pain that began after an MI, cardiac surgery, or catheter procedure 1-6 weeks prior (post-cardiac injury syndrome).
New unexplained CRP elevation above 20 mg/L with low-grade fever and recent chest pain in a young adult without coronary risk factors.
PR segment depression on a routine ECG without chest pain — may precede or accompany subclinical pericardial inflammation.
emergencySigns: Hypotension with distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds, and tachycardia (Beck's triad) — cardiac tamponade from rapidly accumulating pericardial effusion requiring immediate pericardiocentesis.
Pulsus paradoxus greater than 10 mmHg (fall in systolic BP with inspiration) in a patient with known pericardial effusion — a sensitive sign of tamponade physiology.
High fever (above 39°C) with a toxic appearance, leukocytosis above 20×10⁹/L, and rapidly accumulating effusion — purulent pericarditis requiring emergency drainage and IV antibiotics.
ST elevation in two contiguous leads with high troponin in the context of acute chest pain — excludes STEMI before concluding pericarditis; cardiac MRI helps differentiate myo-pericarditis from MI.

causes.description: Enteroviruses (Coxsackie B), adenovirus, echovirus, influenza, EBV, CMV, and SARS-CoV-2 are the most commonly implicated pathogens, although specific viral identification is not achieved in most clinical cases without expensive PCR on pericardial fluid. The mechanism involves direct viral cytopathic effect on pericardial mesothelial cells and immune-mediated inflammation.

Systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic sclerosis, Sjögren's syndrome, ANCA-associated vasculitis, and periodic fever syndromes (FMF, TRAPS, CAPS) all trigger recurrent pericarditis through immune complex deposition or dysregulated inflammasome activation in the pericardium.

Immune-mediated inflammation triggered by myocardial or pericardial cell injury releases cardiac antigens, generating anti-heart antibodies. Occurs 1-6 weeks after acute MI (Dressler syndrome), cardiac surgery, percutaneous coronary intervention, catheter ablation for arrhythmias, or traumatic injury.

Mycobacterium tuberculosis reaches the pericardium via haematogenous spread or direct extension from mediastinal lymph nodes or lungs. Produces a serofibrinous or purulent exudate with high protein and lymphocyte count. Constitutive inflammatory response over months leads to pericardial fibrosis and calcification.

Haematogenous seeding from bacteraemia (Staphylococcus aureus most common), direct extension from adjacent pneumonia or lung abscess, or post-surgical mediastinitis. Less common than viral causes but far higher mortality if not surgically drained within hours.

Secondary malignancy accounts for approximately 5-10% of pericardial disease in cancer patients. Lung cancer, breast cancer, lymphoma, and melanoma most commonly seed the pericardium haematogenously or by direct extension, producing haemorrhagic effusions and symptomatic pericarditis.

riskFactors.description: Men aged 20-50 have 2-3 times higher incidence of acute idiopathic pericarditis than women, possibly due to sex-specific immune response patterns and testosterone-related effects on pericardial inflammation.

Upper respiratory tract infection, gastroenteritis, or flu-like illness in the preceding 1-4 weeks is the most common identifiable precipitant; enteroviral and adenoviral infections are the most frequently associated.
SLE carries a lifetime pericarditis risk of 25-40%; RA, systemic sclerosis, and vasculitides each confer a 5-15% lifetime risk. Pericarditis in SLE is often the presenting manifestation of a flare.

Post-pericardiotomy syndrome occurs in approximately 10-40% of patients after cardiac surgery, catheter ablation, or pacemaker implantation, driven by immune activation from surgical pericardial trauma.

HIV infection increases pericardial disease incidence 5-7 fold, partly through direct HIV pericardial involvement and partly through increased susceptibility to opportunistic infection including tuberculosis and bacterial pericarditis.
After a first episode, recurrence risk is 15-30% without colchicine. Recurrent episodes increase the risk of incessant pericarditis and eventual constrictive or effusive-constrictive pericarditis.

affectedDemographics: Young to middle-aged adults: the majority of idiopathic pericarditis cases occur in men aged 20-50; it is the most common pericardial disease in this age group across developed countries.
Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia: tuberculous pericarditis dominates (60-90% of aetiology) where HIV prevalence is high; this form carries significantly higher morbidity and mortality.
Immunocompromised patients: those with HIV, post-transplant immunosuppression, or haematological malignancy are at higher risk for opportunistic and neoplastic pericarditis.
Patients with connective tissue diseases: SLE (predominantly women), RA, and systemic sclerosis populations have substantially elevated pericarditis incidence.
Post-cardiac surgery patients: pericarditis and post-pericardiotomy syndrome affect approximately 10-40% of patients who have undergone pericardiotomy, with peak onset 1-6 weeks post-operatively.
diagnosisOverview: Acute pericarditis is a clinical diagnosis requiring at least two of four cardinal criteria: characteristic pericarditic chest pain, pericardial friction rub, new widespread ECG changes, or new/worsening pericardial effusion. The ECG is the most useful initial test and evolves through four classic stages: Stage I shows diffuse saddle-shaped (concave upward) ST elevation in most leads except aVR and V1 (where ST is depressed), with PR depression, distinguishing pericarditis from STEMI's focal, convex ST elevation in a coronary territory. Echocardiography confirms pericardial effusion size and excludes cardiac tamponade. CRP above 30 mg/L supports active inflammation and is used to guide duration of anti-inflammatory treatment — normalisation of CRP before stopping therapy reduces recurrence. Troponin elevation occurs in 30-50% of acute pericarditis cases from epicardial extension (myo-pericarditis) and does not necessarily indicate a worse prognosis if regional wall-motion abnormality is absent on echocardiography. Cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement is the gold standard for confirming myo-pericarditis: pericardial enhancement indicates active inflammation, while epicardial LGE identifies myocardial involvement. Pericardiocentesis is reserved for large effusions causing tamponade, diagnostic uncertainty (suspected bacterial or neoplastic aetiology), or failure to respond to 1 week of anti-inflammatory therapy. Aetiological investigation beyond standard blood tests is low-yield in first-episode idiopathic presentations but warranted in high-risk features: fever above 38.5°C, subacute onset, large effusion, immunosuppression, anti-coagulation, or failure to respond.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies characteristic saddle-shaped ST elevation and PR depression in acute pericarditis; monitors evolution across four stages
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10 electrodes applied in 1 minute. Results immediate. Stage I changes (diffuse ST elevation, PR depression) are present in 60-80% of acute pericarditis cases at initial presentation. Serial ECGs over 48-72 hours confirm the evolving pattern.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies pericardial effusion size, detects tamponade physiology, assesses LV function and wall-motion abnormality for myocarditis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless ultrasound scan taking 20-30 minutes. No preparation needed. Performed same day to exclude haemodynamically significant effusion. Categorises effusion as small (<10 mm), moderate (10-20 mm), or large (>20 mm).

diagnosticTests.test: C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms active inflammation; CRP normalisation guides duration of treatment and timing of return to physical activity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 2-4 hours. CRP typically above 30-50 mg/L in acute pericarditis. Serial CRP every 1-2 weeks until normalised before stopping anti-inflammatory therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies concurrent myocardial involvement (myo-pericarditis); does not by itself change management if wall motion is normal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 30-60 minutes. Troponin rise occurs in 30-50% of acute pericarditis from epicardial irritation. Cardiac MRI is needed if troponin is markedly elevated.

diagnosticTests.test: Cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for diagnosing myo-pericarditis; quantifies pericardial inflammation and myocardial fibrosis; guides return-to-sport decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 45-60 minutes lying in the MRI bore with IV gadolinium contrast injected via cannula. Performed within 1-2 weeks of presentation in myocarditis concern. Pericardial late gadolinium enhancement confirms inflammation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for systemic autoimmune disease in pericarditis associated with rash, arthritis, serositis, or recurrent disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting or non-fasting blood draw; results within 3-7 days. Ordered selectively for clinical features suggesting SLE, RA, or vasculitis, not routinely in all first-episode pericarditis.

treatmentOverview: The 2015 ESC guidelines on pericardial diseases recommend aspirin or NSAIDs as the cornerstone of acute pericarditis treatment, combined with colchicine for all cases without a contraindication. Aspirin 750-1000 mg every 8 hours or ibuprofen 600 mg every 8 hours is given for 1-2 weeks in idiopathic cases, tapering over 2-4 weeks guided by CRP normalisation. Colchicine 0.5 mg twice daily (0.5 mg once daily for weight <70 kg or intolerance) is added for 3 months and has been shown in the COPE trial to halve the recurrence rate from 32% to 11% and in the CORP-2 trial to reduce recurrence of the first recurrence from 24% to 8%. Gastric protection with a proton pump inhibitor is recommended for all patients on NSAIDs for more than one week. Corticosteroids are reserved for cases where NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated or failed (pregnancy, renal failure, NSAID allergy), specific aetiologies requiring steroids (autoimmune, tuberculous with steroids adjunct), or PCIS; low-dose prednisolone (0.2-0.5 mg/kg/day) is used to avoid the rebound and recurrence risk associated with higher doses. Physical activity restriction until symptom resolution and CRP normalisation is mandatory — at least 3 months for competitive athletes (ESC 2015). For recurrent or refractory pericarditis, anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) and rilonacept (IL-1α/β inhibitor) have demonstrated efficacy in randomised trials. Rilonacept received FDA approval in 2021 for recurrent pericarditis based on the RHAPSODY trial (PMID 34058527). Pericardiectomy is a last resort for refractory cases and constrictive pericarditis.

medicalTreatments.name: Aspirin (750-1000 mg every 8 hours, taper over 2-4 weeks) or Ibuprofen (600 mg every 8 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anti-inflammatory agent for acute pericarditis. Aspirin preferred in post-MI pericarditis (Dressler syndrome) to avoid interference with anti-platelet COX-1 inhibition. Ibuprofen preferred in autoimmune disease for its more favourable cardiovascular profile. Taper guided by CRP normalisation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in 70-90% within 1-2 weeks; recurrence without colchicine approximately 20-30%

medicalTreatments.name: Colchicine (0.5 mg twice daily for 3 months; 0.5 mg once daily if weight <70 kg)
medicalTreatments.description: Microtubule inhibitor blocking inflammasome activation and neutrophil migration. Added to aspirin or NSAID for all acute pericarditis. Reduces recurrence from approximately 32% to 11% (COPE trial). Continued for 3 months; longer courses (6 months) for recurrent disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: COPE trial: colchicine + NSAID vs NSAID alone reduced recurrence from 32% to 11% (p=0.004); CORP-2: 24% vs 8% for recurrent pericarditis (p=0.004)

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose prednisolone (0.2-0.5 mg/kg/day, tapered over 4-6 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for NSAID/colchicine failure, contraindications, or specific aetiologies (autoimmune, PCIS). Low dose preferred to avoid recurrence risk associated with higher doses. Always combined with colchicine. Slow taper to avoid rebound.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for symptom relief; however, a 3-fold higher recurrence rate compared with NSAID-based therapy in observational studies — used as last resort for idiopathic pericarditis

medicalTreatments.name: Anakinra (100 mg subcutaneous daily, typically 8-12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Recombinant IL-1 receptor antagonist blocking interleukin-1-mediated pericardial inflammation. Used for colchicine-resistant or corticosteroid-dependent recurrent pericarditis. Also used in CAPS and other autoinflammatory syndromes causing recurrent serositis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AIRTRIP trial (PMID 27402237): anakinra vs placebo in corticosteroid-dependent recurrent pericarditis — recurrence in 7% vs 73% (p<0.001)

medicalTreatments.name: Rilonacept (320 mg loading SC, then 160 mg SC weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: IL-1α/β trap approved by FDA in March 2021 for recurrent pericarditis. Reduces pericardial inflammation by neutralising circulating IL-1α and IL-1β. Given weekly by subcutaneous injection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: RHAPSODY trial: rilonacept vs placebo in colchicine-eligible recurrent pericarditis — pericarditis recurrence hazard ratio 0.04 (96% reduction, p<0.001)

surgicalOptions.name: Pericardiocentesis (pericardial drainage)
surgicalOptions.description: Needle or catheter aspiration of pericardial fluid under echocardiographic or fluoroscopic guidance. Performed for cardiac tamponade, large symptomatic effusion, or diagnostic sampling of suspected bacterial or neoplastic pericarditis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success rate over 95% in experienced centres; recurrence of effusion requiring repeat drainage approximately 10-20% for malignant effusions; perioperative mortality under 1% for elective cases

surgicalOptions.name: Pericardiectomy (surgical stripping of the pericardium)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the pericardium for constrictive pericarditis (end-stage fibrotic sequela) or for refractory recurrent pericarditis not responding to maximal medical therapy including IL-1 inhibitors. Performed via median sternotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-80% of constrictive pericarditis cases; 5-year survival approximately 70-80%; early surgical referral before severe right heart failure improves outcomes

alternativeTreatments.name: Physical activity restriction
alternativeTreatments.description: Mandatory rest until symptoms resolve and CRP normalises — typically 2-4 weeks for non-athletes. Competitive athletes must wait at least 3 months with documented clinical recovery, normal ECG, echocardiogram, and CRP before resuming sports. Evidence from consensus guidelines shows that premature return to exercise worsens outcomes and increases recurrence risk.

whySeeSpecialist: A cardiologist should evaluate all patients with suspected acute pericarditis to confirm the diagnosis, exclude myocarditis and cardiac tamponade with echocardiography, and guide anti-inflammatory therapy. Recurrent or refractory cases, large effusions, and high-risk features warrant specialist cardiac care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a general cardiologist or cardiac imaging specialist for initial assessment including echocardiography and cardiac MRI if myocarditis is suspected. For refractory or recurrent pericarditis requiring anakinra or rilonacept, a cardiologist or rheumatologist experienced with autoinflammatory disease biologics provides optimal management.
hospitalCriteria: 24/7 echocardiography for assessment of pericardial effusion and tamponade
Access to cardiac MRI for suspected myo-pericarditis
Pericardiocentesis capability (interventional cardiology or cardiac surgery) for haemodynamic compromise
Colchicine and NSAIDs availability; access to IL-1 inhibitors for refractory disease
Cardiac surgery for pericardiectomy when indicated
keyFacilities: Tertiary cardiology centres with cardiac imaging and pericardiocentesis capability
Chest pain assessment units for initial triage and ECG interpretation
Rheumatology services for autoimmune aetiology evaluation and biologic prescribing
preventionStrategies: Always complete the prescribed 3-month course of colchicine after a first episode; premature discontinuation increases recurrence from ~8% to ~32%.
Avoid anti-inflammatory drug tapering until CRP has fully normalised — premature dose reduction is the most common cause of symptom rebound.
Restrict physical activity and contact sports for at least 3 months after a first episode and longer if CRP remains elevated.
In patients with known autoimmune disease, optimise disease-modifying therapy to reduce serositis triggers.
Treat underlying infections (tuberculosis, viral) promptly to reduce pericardial inflammation duration and risk of constrictive sequelae.
lifestyleModifications: Rest from strenuous exercise and competitive sports until CRP is normal and symptoms have fully resolved — at minimum 2-4 weeks for the general population and 3 months for competitive athletes.
Avoid alcohol during NSAID or colchicine therapy: alcohol worsens gastrointestinal side-effects and may exacerbate pericardial inflammation.
Take colchicine with food if gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhoea is the most common dose-limiting side-effect) occur.
Monitor for ankle swelling and worsening breathlessness that may indicate recurrent effusion or developing constriction.
Attend follow-up appointments at 2-4 weeks, 6 weeks, and 3 months; carry an updated ECG to compare with any future presentations.
Report recurrent chest pain immediately rather than self-managing with GTN (which does not relieve pericarditic pain).

recommended: Anti-inflammatory dietary pattern with oily fish, olive oil, colourful vegetables, and whole grains to reduce systemic inflammation
Adequate hydration (2+ litres water daily) to reduce risk of NSAID-related renal impairment
High-fibre foods to reduce constipation risk from colchicine-altered gut motility
Probiotics or yogurt during antibiotic therapy if bacterial superinfection is being treated
avoid: Alcohol during NSAID and colchicine therapy (worsens GI side-effects and may impair anti-inflammatory effect)
NSAIDs on an empty stomach (take with food or a PPI to reduce gastric ulceration risk)
Grapefruit juice if taking colchicine (inhibits CYP3A4, raising colchicine plasma levels and toxicity risk)
High-sodium processed foods if on corticosteroids (steroids cause fluid retention; low sodium diet reduces blood pressure and oedema)

exerciseGuidelines: Strict rest from competitive sports and vigorous exercise until symptom-free and CRP normal. For non-athletes this typically means 2-4 weeks of activity restriction. Competitive athletes must meet all of: no symptoms, normal ECG, normal CRP, normal echocardiogram, and absence of complex arrhythmia on Holter monitoring before returning to full training. The 3-month minimum restriction for athletes is based on ESC 2015 consensus given evidence that exercise during active pericardial inflammation accelerates recurrence and may trigger arrhythmia.
dailyManagement: Take NSAIDs with food at scheduled times to maintain anti-inflammatory levels; use a PPI concurrently.
Take colchicine at the same time each day for 3 months; do not stop early even when feeling well.
Check temperature daily in the first 2 weeks; persistent fever above 38°C for more than 7 days on treatment warrants medical review.
Monitor stool for unusual changes (colchicine-related diarrhoea) and reduce dose temporarily if severe.
Measure and record resting heart rate and blood pressure weekly; bradycardia or increasing dyspnoea may signal effusion growth.
Attend CRP blood test appointments at 2-4 weeks and 6-8 weeks to confirm inflammation is resolving.
prognosis: The outlook for idiopathic or viral acute pericarditis is excellent. Over 70-80% of patients are free of recurrence at 12 months with aspirin plus colchicine. Recurrence occurs in approximately 15-30% without colchicine and 8-11% with colchicine (COPE trial). Of those who recur, a small proportion develop incessant or multiple recurrences requiring colchicine continuation, IL-1 inhibitor therapy, or eventually pericardiectomy. Progression to constrictive pericarditis is rare after idiopathic or viral pericarditis (under 1%) but occurs in 20-40% of untreated tuberculous pericarditis. Cardiac tamponade complicates approximately 5-15% of first-episode pericarditis overall and up to 60% of neoplastic pericarditis. Myo-pericarditis with troponin elevation but normal LV function has a similarly excellent prognosis; significant LV dysfunction raises long-term risk of dilated cardiomyopathy.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients improve within 1-2 weeks of starting aspirin or NSAID plus colchicine. CRP typically normalises by 2-4 weeks in uncomplicated cases. Physical activity restriction continues until full clinical and biochemical recovery. Return to competitive sport: minimum 3 months. Colchicine continues for 3 months in first episode and up to 6 months in recurrence. Full clinical review at 3 months to confirm resolution and discuss prevention.
complications: Recurrent pericarditis: the most common complication, occurring in 15-30% without colchicine, with each recurrence increasing the risk of further episodes and incessant disease.
Cardiac tamponade: haemodynamically significant pericardial effusion causing cardiogenic shock; requires emergency pericardiocentesis; most common in large neoplastic or bacterial effusions.

Constrictive pericarditis: fibrous, calcified pericardium limits diastolic ventricular filling causing right heart failure and ascites; requires surgical pericardiectomy; predominantly a complication of tuberculous pericarditis.
Effusive-constrictive pericarditis: simultaneous effusion with early pericardial constriction; does not fully resolve after pericardiocentesis; managed with prolonged anti-inflammatory therapy.
Myo-pericarditis: epicardial extension of inflammation causes troponin elevation and, rarely, LV dysfunction; significant cardiomyopathy develops in under 5% of acute myo-pericarditis cases.
Atrial arrhythmias: atrial fibrillation and flutter arise from pericardial inflammation adjacent to the atria, particularly with large effusions; usually self-limited once pericarditis resolves.

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — Pericarditis
supportResources.description: Patient guide on pericarditis types, symptoms, medications, and activity restrictions.

supportResources.name: Myocarditis Foundation — Pericarditis Information
supportResources.description: Patient community and educational resources for pericarditis and myo-pericarditis, including return-to-sport guidance.

supportResources.name: ESC Patient Information — Pericardial Disease
supportResources.description: European Society of Cardiology patient-facing guidance on pericarditis management and monitoring.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MI causes pressure-like substernal pain that is worse at rest and unchanged with posture, with focal ST elevation in a coronary territory and a rise-and-fall pattern of troponin from myocardial necrosis. Pericarditis causes sharp pleuritic pain relieved by sitting forward, with diffuse concave ST elevation and PR depression across multiple leads; wall-motion abnormality on echo favours MI over pericarditis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Myocarditis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Myocarditis presents with chest pain, dyspnoea, and markedly elevated troponin with new LV systolic dysfunction or wall-motion abnormality on echocardiography. Cardiac MRI shows myocardial LGE in a non-coronary distribution. Pericarditis involves the pericardial sac with smaller troponin rises and typically preserved LV function when myocardial involvement is limited.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thoracic Aortic Dissection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aortic dissection presents with tearing back pain of maximal severity at onset, unequal arm blood pressures, and widened mediastinum on X-ray. Pericarditis causes pleuritic, postural chest pain over hours to days with characteristic ECG changes. CT angiography is required when dissection cannot be excluded clinically.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Embolism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulmonary embolism causes sudden-onset pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea without the postural improvement of pericarditis; ECG shows sinus tachycardia or S1Q3T3 pattern, not diffuse ST elevation. D-dimer and CT pulmonary angiography confirm or exclude PE.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Myocarditis
relatedConditions.relevance: Myo-pericarditis occurs when pericardial inflammation extends to the adjacent epicardium and myocardium; both conditions share viral triggers and are diagnosed together on cardiac MRI.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-MI pericarditis (Dressler syndrome) is a recognised autoimmune complication of transmural MI occurring 1-4 weeks after the infarct.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: SLE is the most common autoimmune cause of pericarditis, occurring in 25-40% of SLE patients during their disease course.

relatedConditions.name: Heart Failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Constrictive pericarditis after recurrent or tuberculous pericarditis mimics right heart failure; differentiating the two with cardiac MRI or haemodynamic catheterisation has major therapeutic implications.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of acute pericarditis?
faqs.answer: The hallmark is sharp, stabbing chest pain that worsens with breathing in, coughing, and lying flat, and improves when sitting forward. Pain often radiates to the left shoulder or trapezius ridge. Fever, fatigue, and a fast heart rate are common. A pericardial friction rub (scratching sound) may be heard with a stethoscope.

faqs.question: How is acute pericarditis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires at least two of: typical pleuritic chest pain, a pericardial friction rub, characteristic ECG changes (diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation and PR depression), or pericardial effusion on echocardiography. CRP confirms active inflammation. Cardiac MRI identifies myocardial involvement (myo-pericarditis) when troponin is elevated.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pericarditis and a heart attack?
faqs.answer: Pericarditis causes sharp pleuritic pain relieved by leaning forward, with diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation across most ECG leads and PR depression. A heart attack causes sustained crushing chest pain unrelated to breathing, with focal ST elevation in a coronary territory and a rising troponin from myocardial cell death. Echocardiography distinguishes the two by showing wall-motion abnormality in MI but not typically in pericarditis.

faqs.question: How is pericarditis treated?
faqs.answer: Standard treatment is aspirin or ibuprofen for 1-2 weeks combined with colchicine for 3 months. Colchicine halves the recurrence rate. A proton pump inhibitor protects the stomach during NSAID therapy. Corticosteroids are reserved for cases where NSAIDs are contraindicated. Physical activity is restricted until symptoms and CRP fully resolve.

faqs.question: Can pericarditis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence occurs in approximately 15-30% of patients after a first episode without colchicine and in about 10% when colchicine is taken for 3 months. Multiple recurrences require longer colchicine courses; biologic IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra or rilonacept) are effective for colchicine-resistant recurrent pericarditis.

faqs.question: How long does pericarditis last?
faqs.answer: Most patients with acute idiopathic pericarditis improve within 1-2 weeks of treatment. CRP normalises within 2-4 weeks. Colchicine is continued for 3 months to prevent recurrence. Incessant pericarditis persists beyond 4-6 weeks; chronic pericarditis lasts more than 3 months and requires specialist evaluation.

faqs.question: Does pericarditis affect the heart permanently?
faqs.answer: Viral or idiopathic pericarditis rarely causes permanent heart damage; under 1% progress to constrictive pericarditis. Tuberculous pericarditis has a 20-40% risk of constrictive disease if untreated. Myo-pericarditis rarely leads to dilated cardiomyopathy if LV function is preserved at diagnosis.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency department for pericarditis?
faqs.answer: Go to the ED immediately if chest pain is severe and unrelieved by sitting forward, if you feel faint or dizzy (signs of cardiac tamponade), if you develop progressive breathlessness at rest, or if you have high fever (above 39°C) suggesting bacterial infection. These indicate potentially life-threatening complications.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with pericarditis?
faqs.answer: No strenuous exercise or competitive sports until symptoms resolve and CRP is normal — minimum 2-4 weeks for non-athletes. Competitive athletes must wait at least 3 months with confirmed full clinical and biochemical recovery. Premature return to exercise worsens outcomes and increases recurrence risk.

faqs.question: What ECG changes does pericarditis cause?
faqs.answer: Pericarditis causes diffuse saddle-shaped (concave upward) ST elevation in most limb and precordial leads except aVR and V1. PR depression in the same leads reflects atrial involvement. These diffuse changes across multiple lead groups distinguish pericarditis from the focal, coronary-territory ST elevation of STEMI.

faqs.question: What causes most cases of pericarditis?
faqs.answer: Most cases in Western countries are idiopathic or viral, accounting for 70-90% of presentations. Common viral triggers include enteroviruses (Coxsackie B), adenovirus, influenza, EBV, and CMV. In sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, Mycobacterium tuberculosis is the dominant cause. Autoimmune diseases, post-cardiac injury, and cancer are other important causes.

faqs.question: Is colchicine necessary for pericarditis?
faqs.answer: Yes, for nearly all patients. The COPE trial showed colchicine added to NSAIDs reduced recurrence from 32% to 11%. ESC 2015 guidelines recommend colchicine for all acute and recurrent pericarditis unless contraindicated. The standard duration is 3 months for a first episode.

faqs.question: What is cardiac tamponade?
faqs.answer: Cardiac tamponade is compression of the heart by rapidly accumulating pericardial fluid, preventing adequate filling. It presents with hypotension, neck vein distension, and muffled heart sounds (Beck's triad). Pulsus paradoxus above 10 mmHg is a sensitive sign. Emergency pericardiocentesis (needle drainage of the sac) is lifesaving.

faqs.question: Can pericarditis lead to constrictive pericarditis?
faqs.answer: Rarely after idiopathic or viral pericarditis — under 1% of cases. Tuberculous pericarditis carries a 20-40% risk without appropriate treatment. Constrictive pericarditis causes right heart failure with ascites, leg oedema, and dyspnoea and requires surgical pericardiectomy.

faqs.question: What is pericardial friction rub?
faqs.answer: A pericardial friction rub is a high-pitched, scratching, multi-component sound heard on auscultation at the left lower sternal border, caused by the two inflamed pericardial layers rubbing together. It is pathognomonic of pericarditis when present but is intermittent — a single absence on examination does not exclude the diagnosis.

metaDescription: Acute pericarditis: sharp pleuritic chest pain from heart sac inflammation. ECG changes, colchicine treatment, recurrence prevention reviewed by cardiologists.
